scan.agent: Hermes Agent (with Hermes Desktop operator layer)

intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Hermes Agent is a single-tenant personal AI assistant that converses with one operator over a terminal UI and messaging gateways (Telegram, Discord, Slack, Signal, email, SMS, and others), and acts on the operator's behalf through a documented toolset — `terminal`, `code_execution`, `file`, `web`, `browser`, `memory`, `skills`, `delegation`, `cronjob`, `messaging` — while curating its own memory and skills. It must act only for its authorized operator, keep dangerous shell and code behind the OS-level isolation and operator-approval controls its documentation describes, and never exfiltrate operator credentials or session-authorization material outside the trust envelope. The combined remit also covers Hermes Desktop, a non-LLM Electron operator layer that installs, configures, and drives an Agent instance locally, over a plain Remote HTTP connection, or over an SSH tunnel that must bind loopback-only, authenticate key-only as a dedicated non-root user, and pin the remote host key.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Two workspaces analyzed as one agent. The Agent is a large Python codebase (`gateway/`, `tools/`, pluggable terminal backends in `tools/environments/` — `local.py`, `docker.py`, `ssh.py`, `modal.py`, etc.) whose default `local` backend runs `terminal()` shell and the file tools directly on the host; the approval gate (`tools/approval.py`), credential env-scrubbing (`tools/env_passthrough.py`, `tools/environments/local.py`), MCP launch path (`tools/mcp_tool.py` + `tools/osv_check.py`), Skills Guard (`tools/skills_guard.py`), and rotating redacted logging (`hermes_logging.py`) form an in-process control layer the project's own `SECURITY.md` §2 explicitly labels "not a boundary." The Desktop is an Electron/TypeScript app (`src/main/ssh-tunnel.ts`, `ssh-remote.ts`, `ssh-options.ts`, `installer.ts`, `config.ts`, renderer `utils/analytics.ts`); it brokers operator control over SSH (loopback-bound, BatchMode key-only, `accept-new` host-key TOFU) and ships PostHog telemetry default-on in official builds. The repo ships a candid `SECURITY.md`, exact-pinned (`==`) Python dependencies with a hash-locked `uv.lock`, and supply-chain CI (OSV scanner, lockfile check, supply-chain audit).

behavior_summary:
This is a high-maturity, actively-maintained single-tenant agent whose security model is honest about its own limits: its own SECURITY.md states plainly that the only boundary against an adversarial LLM is the operating system, and that the approval gate, output redaction, Skills Guard, and tool allowlists are heuristics, not containment. The dominant risk is the gap between that stated model and the default deployment: the local terminal backend runs LLM-emitted shell and file operations directly on the host, while untrusted content (open web, inbound email, multi-user gateways, operator-configured MCP servers) flows into the same agent context — so a single indirect prompt injection reaches host execution unless the operator has separately stood up Docker/OpenShell whole-process isolation that the code does not require by default.

Layered on that are second-order gaps the team has largely disclosed: dangerous-shell auto-approval fails open in non-interactive/non-gateway/non-cron execution, messaging adapters default to open/pair when no caller allowlist is set, MCP servers launch and their tools execute with no operator-review or approval gate, and the Desktop ships default-on telemetry with no non-root SSH guard and trust-on-first-use host keys. These sit against genuinely strong, operative controls — a fail-closed API-server adapter, an un-overridable credential blocklist (the GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf fix), a hardline command floor below YOLO mode, exact-pinned deps with supply-chain CI, and redacting rotating logs — which is why the posture lands at the Established/Partial boundary rather than lower.

rules.rule_text: Every enabled network-exposed adapter MUST refuse to dispatch agent work, resolve approvals, or relay output until an operator-configured caller allowlist is set; no adapter may fail open when no allowlist is configured.
Authorization MUST be re-checked at every surface that crosses a trust boundary; a session identifier is a routing handle only and MUST NOT be accepted as proof of authorization.
Local-IPC and editor surfaces (ACP, TUI gateway) MUST rely on OS-level access control and MUST NOT be exposed beyond the local user without an explicit network authentication layer.
Binding a loopback-default HTTP surface to a non-loopback interface MUST be an explicit operator action, never a silent default.
The SSH Tunnel MUST bind only to `127.0.0.1` on the desktop side and MUST NOT expose the remote Hermes port to the public internet.
The desktop MUST authenticate to a remote Hermes host using key-based SSH in non-interactive (`BatchMode`) mode, and MUST NOT fall back to transmitting an operator password to satisfy a prompt.
The desktop MUST verify the remote host key on first connection and pin it, and MUST fail closed if a pinned host key later changes rather than silently re-trusting it.
The desktop MUST authorize its connection only against a dedicated, non-root Hermes user account on the remote host.

rules.section: Data Boundaries — Forbidden Data Movement — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Provider API keys and gateway tokens MUST be stripped from the environment passed to lower-trust in-process components (shell subprocesses, MCP subprocesses, the code-execution child) except where the operator or a loaded skill has explicitly declared a passthrough variable.

rules.section: Data Boundaries — Forbidden Data Movement — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Secret-like patterns MUST be redacted from operator-facing display surfaces.

rules.section: Data Boundaries — Forbidden Data Movement — Hermes Desktop
rules.rule_text: Operator-entered credentials (provider API keys, SSH key paths, remote API keys) MUST be stored only in the operator's own config files and MUST NOT be transmitted anywhere other than the configured Agent host.

rules.section: Restricted Tools (Require Approval Before Use) — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Shell execution via the `terminal` tool MUST be subject to the operator approval path before a destructive command runs; auto-approval of shell beyond the operator's configured allowlist is not authorized.

rules.section: Requires Human Approval Before Execution — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Installation of third-party skills and plugins, which MUST be subject to operator review (reading the skill's Python and scripts, not only its description) before the code is loaded and run.

rules.section: Requires Human Approval Before Execution — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Loading or launching an operator-configured MCP server, which MUST pass the documented supply-chain guard at launch.

Ingesting content from operator-uncontrolled surfaces (open web, inbound email, multi-user channels, untrusted MCP servers) while relying only on in-process heuristics, with no OS-level isolation boundary in force.
Running the agent process as root in a deployment that ingests untrusted input.
SSH'ing into, or authorizing its key on, the remote `root` account.
Disabling the Agent's host-key verification or weakening the SSH tunnel's loopback-only bind.

rules.section: Behavioral Expectations — Acceptable Retry Behavior — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: The tool-calling loop MUST remain bounded by its iteration and budget limits and MUST stop on an operator interrupt.

rules.section: Behavioral Expectations — Acceptable Retry Behavior — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Cron sessions MUST honor the documented hard interrupt; a job that fails repeatedly MUST be auto-blocked rather than retried indefinitely.
Acting as an autonomous agent or independently initiating agentic actions — it brokers operator control and must not originate tool calls or shell execution of its own.

rules.section: Tools and Capabilities — Forbidden Data Movement — Hermes Agent
rules.rule_text: Credentials and session-authorization material MUST NOT leak to any destination outside the trust envelope through environment passthrough, adapter logging, or transport errors that flush them upstream.

findings.summary: Default `local` terminal backend executes LLM-emitted shell on the host while untrusted web/email/MCP content is ingested with only in-process heuristics in force.
findings.description: The default terminal backend runs `terminal()` shell and the file tools directly on the host, and untrusted content from the open web, inbound email, multi-user gateways, and operator-configured MCP servers enters the same agent context — so one indirect prompt injection reaches host execution unless the operator has separately deployed Docker/OpenShell whole-process isolation. The remit's own SECURITY.md §2.2 names the OS as the only boundary and §2.4 labels the approval gate, redaction, and Skills Guard heuristics, not containment; the code defaults to no OS boundary. This is the enabling condition for the auto-approval (PRAX-002) and ungated-MCP (PRAX-004) chains.
findings.policy_rule_text: Ingesting content from operator-uncontrolled surfaces (open web, inbound email, multi-user channels, untrusted MCP servers) while relying only on in-process heuristics, with no OS-level isolation boundary in force.

evidence.snippet: Default terminal backend; runs LLM-emitted shell and file tools (read_file/write_file/patch) directly on the host with no container/sandbox between the model output and the host.

"The only security boundary against an adversarial LLM is the operating system. Nothing inside the agent process constitutes containment" — §2.2, with §2.4 naming approval/redaction/Skills Guard as heuristics.

recommended_actions: Default to a non-`local` terminal backend (or whole-process Docker/OpenShell wrapping) whenever any untrusted input surface (gateway, email, web, MCP) is enabled, and refuse to ingest those surfaces under the `local` backend without an explicit operator break-glass acknowledgement.
Surface the chosen isolation posture in the runtime banner/logs so an operator running `local` with untrusted surfaces is warned at startup.

findings.summary: Dangerous-command approval fails open — flagged shell is auto-approved in non-interactive, non-gateway, non-cron execution contexts.
findings.description: When a command matches a dangerous pattern but the session is neither CLI-interactive, gateway, nor cron, the approval path logs a warning and returns approved rather than blocking. Any execution context that does not set `HERMES_INTERACTIVE`/`HERMES_GATEWAY_SESSION`/`HERMES_CRON_SESSION` — e.g. an embedded or programmatic invocation — silently bypasses the operator approval the remit requires for destructive shell.
findings.policy_rule_text: Shell execution via the `terminal` tool MUST be subject to the operator approval path before a destructive command runs; auto-approval of shell beyond the operator's configured allowlist is not authorized.

evidence.snippet: "AUTO-APPROVED dangerous command in non-interactive non-gateway context" — logger.warning then `return {"approved": True}` at :988 when not is_cli and not is_gateway (and cron not in deny mode).
Mirror path in the tirith/decide flow — `if not is_cli and not is_gateway and not is_ask:` returns approved (:1223) outside cron-deny.

recommended_actions: Fail closed in unknown contexts: default the non-interactive/non-gateway/non-cron branch to deny (or require an explicit opt-in env flag to auto-approve) so a dangerous command is never executed without an approval surface.

findings.summary: Several messaging adapters fail open — they default to open/pair/allow-all policy when no operator caller allowlist is configured.
findings.description: The remit and SECURITY.md §2.6 require every network-exposed adapter to refuse work until an allowlist is set, but WhatsApp and Yuanbao default to `dm_policy/group_policy="open"`, Signal's DM allowlist defaults to `"*"` (allow-all), and the unauthorized-DM default is `"pair"` on an open gateway. An operator who enables one of these adapters without explicitly configuring an allowlist gets a surface that dispatches agent work for unauthenticated callers.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every enabled network-exposed adapter MUST refuse to dispatch agent work, resolve approvals, or relay output until an operator-configured caller allowlist is set; no adapter may fail open when no allowlist is configured.

evidence.snippet: SIGNAL_ALLOWED_USERS defaults to "*" (allow-all) when unset; whatsapp.py:236-239 and yuanbao.py:1520-1531 default dm/group policy to "open".
Unauthorized-DM default resolves to "pair" (open-gateway) when no allowlist configured, rather than refusing to dispatch.

recommended_actions: Change the no-allowlist default on every network-exposed adapter to refuse-and-warn (fail closed), matching the API server adapter's behavior, and require an explicit operator `--allow-anyone` style opt-in for open policy.

findings.summary: Operator-configured MCP servers launch automatically and their tools execute with no operator-review or approval gate.
findings.description: `discover_mcp_tools()` connects to every server with `enabled` truthy at startup with no approval/review step, and the registered MCP tools — which can include exec/delete/send capabilities — are callable without routing through `tools/approval.py` (which contains zero MCP handling). The remit requires MCP loads to pass the documented supply-chain guard and high-impact tool use to be approval-gated; launch-time vetting is limited to a fail-open malware-only OSV check (PRAX-010) and a log-only description scanner (PRAX-008).
findings.policy_rule_text: Loading or launching an operator-configured MCP server, which MUST pass the documented supply-chain guard at launch.

evidence.snippet: discover_mcp_tools() connects every server gated only by the `enabled` flag — no operator approval/confirm step before launch.
MCP tools auto-register and are exposed/callable; tools/approval.py has no MCP references so exec/delete/send MCP tools run without per-call approval.

recommended_actions: Require an explicit operator review/confirm step before a newly-configured MCP server is launched, and route high-impact MCP tool invocations (exec/delete/send) through the same approval gate as the `terminal` tool.

findings.summary: MCP tool-description poisoning scanner is advisory only — it logs a warning but never blocks the poisoned tool from being exposed to the model.
findings.description: `_scan_mcp_description()` matches a useful set of injection patterns (instruction overrides, role tags, network/exec references) but on a hit only emits a `logger.warning`, and the call site discards the return value so the tool is still registered and reaches the model context. A poisoned MCP tool description therefore influences the model with no enforcement.
findings.policy_rule_text: Loading or launching an operator-configured MCP server, which MUST pass the documented supply-chain guard at launch.

evidence.snippet: "These are WARNING-level — we log but don't block"; _scan_mcp_description logger.warning at :379, return value discarded at the :3260 call site.

recommended_actions: Escalate a description-scan hit to at least an operator confirmation (or omit the tool pending review) rather than registering it silently; record the hit to the structured session log, not only the rotating warning log.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop ships PostHog telemetry default-on (opt-out) in official builds via a CI-injected key.
findings.description: `isAnalyticsEnabled()` returns enabled on first run whenever a PostHog key is baked in, and the release workflow injects `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` from a CI secret into official builds — so production Desktop builds send telemetry by default unless the operator opts out. Captured data is version/platform/feature-usage metadata with session recording disabled and DNT respected, and credentials are not transmitted, so the exposure is metadata not secrets — but the default-on posture on an official build was not declared in the remit's data boundaries.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operator-entered credentials (provider API keys, SSH key paths, remote API keys) MUST be stored only in the operator's own config files and MUST NOT be transmitted anywhere other than the configured Agent host.

evidence.snippet: isAnalyticsEnabled() — `if (stored === null) return hasKey` → enabled by default whenever VITE_POSTHOG_KEY is present (opt-out).
VITE_POSTHOG_KEY injected from CI secret into official release builds, so shipped builds carry the key and default telemetry on.

recommended_actions: Make telemetry opt-in (default disabled until the operator consents) for official builds, or surface a first-run consent prompt before the first `posthog.capture`.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop has no non-root remote-user guard — the operator may configure the SSH connection to authorize as the remote `root` account.
findings.description: The remote SSH username is operator-supplied free text stored and used verbatim with no validation, and the gateway-start logic explicitly anticipates a root SSH user. The remit forbids the Desktop from SSH'ing into or authorizing its key on the remote `root` account and requires a dedicated non-root Hermes user; nothing in the code enforces that.
findings.policy_rule_text: The desktop MUST authorize its connection only against a dedicated, non-root Hermes user account on the remote host. / SSH'ing into, or authorizing its key on, the remote `root` account.

evidence.snippet: `username: (ssh.username as string) || ""` — no validation; ssh-remote.ts:40 uses `${config.username}@${config.host}` verbatim, and ssh-remote.ts:1459 comments on the SSH user being root.

recommended_actions: Reject (or hard-warn and require explicit override) when the configured SSH username is `root`, and document the dedicated non-root Hermes user as the supported target.

findings.summary: SSH host-key handling is trust-on-first-use (`accept-new`) — a first connection auto-trusts whatever key is presented.
findings.description: The Desktop sets `StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`, which fails closed on a CHANGED key (good, and the error is surfaced) but auto-accepts the host key on first connection without operator verification, leaving the initial connection open to a MITM that is present at first use. The remit asks for verify-and-pin on first connection; the implementation pins-on-first-use without a verification step.
findings.policy_rule_text: The desktop MUST verify the remote host key on first connection and pin it, and MUST fail closed if a pinned host key later changes rather than silently re-trusting it.

evidence.snippet: StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new (also ssh-remote.ts:32); changed-key failure surfaced at ssh-remote.ts:111-116, but first-use key is accepted without operator confirmation.

recommended_actions: Present the remote host-key fingerprint to the operator for confirmation on first connection before pinning it, rather than silently accepting on first use.

findings.summary: First-run installer fetches and pipes an install script over `curl | bash` from the `main` branch with no checksum or signature pin.
findings.description: The Desktop installer runs `curl -fsSL .../NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/scripts/install.sh | bash` (and the Windows `install.ps1` equivalent) to install the Agent on the host. It is operator-initiated and gated behind a local sudo prompt, but it pulls an unpinned script from a moving branch ref with no integrity verification, so a repo or transport compromise lands as host code execution under the operator.

evidence.snippet: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/scripts/install.sh | bash -s -- --skip-setup` — unpinned `main`, no checksum/signature; Windows path at :1001.

recommended_actions: Pin the installer to a release tag/commit SHA and verify a published checksum or signature before executing, rather than piping `main` directly into a shell.

findings.summary: MCP launch OSV check is malware-only (`MAL-*` advisories) and fail-open on any network or parse error.
findings.description: The launch-time supply-chain guard for MCP packages queries OSV but keeps only `MAL-*` malware advisories, ignoring regular CVEs, and returns allow on any network/parse failure — so a known-vulnerable (but not flagged-malicious) MCP package, or one queried while OSV is unreachable, launches unimpeded. This narrows but does not break the documented launch guard; it is noted so the operator understands the guard's scope.

evidence.snippet: Only MAL-* advisories retained (CVEs ignored); :48-51 returns None (allow) on any network/parse error — fail-open.

recommended_actions: Extend the launch check to flag high-severity CVEs in addition to MAL-* advisories, and treat an OSV query failure as a soft-block-and-prompt rather than silent allow.

positives.title: Un-overridable provider-credential blocklist (GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf fix)
positives.description: Provider API keys and gateway tokens are stripped by default from shell, MCP, and code-execution children, and a hardcoded blocklist cannot be re-enabled as passthrough by either a malicious skill's frontmatter or operator config.yaml — directly closing the GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf sandbox-escape.

positives.title: Fail-closed API server adapter authentication
positives.description: The network-exposed API server adapter refuses to start without an `API_SERVER_KEY`, rejects network-accessible placeholder keys, and uses constant-time bearer comparison returning 401 on mismatch.

positives.title: Hardline command blocklist below YOLO mode
positives.description: A set of catastrophic commands (`rm -rf /`, `mkfs`, `dd of=/dev/sdX`, fork bomb, shutdown/reboot) is unconditionally blocked before the yolo/smart/mode=off gate, so no session-level setting can bypass it.

positives.title: Exact-pinned dependencies with supply-chain CI
positives.description: Python deps are pinned with `==` and a sha256-hash-locked `uv.lock`, enforced by `uv-lockfile-check.yml`, `osv-scanner.yml`, and `supply-chain-audit.yml`.

positives.title: Redacting rotating logs
positives.description: All log files use `RotatingFileHandler` with a `RedactingFormatter` so secret-like strings are stripped before reaching disk, across `agent.log`, `errors.log`, and `gateway.log`.

positives.title: SSH tunnel is loopback-bound, key-only, fail-closed on changed host key
positives.description: The Desktop's SSH tunnel forwards to `127.0.0.1` only, uses `BatchMode=yes` (key-only, no password fallback), and surfaces a hard error when the remote host identification changes.

rows.purpose: primary agent runtime + tool/approval decision log

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Established posture at the Partial boundary. Hermes Agent pairs a coherent, honestly-documented safety model and several genuinely operative controls — strip-by-default credential scoping with an un-overridable blocklist, a hardline command floor, exact-pinned hash-locked dependencies with supply-chain CI, and redacting rotating logs — with a structural Zero-Trust gap it discloses rather than fixes: the default backend executes attacker-influenceable shell on the host with only in-process heuristics in force, and several authorization paths (messaging-adapter allowlists, MCP launch/exec, non-interactive auto-approval) fail open or are ungated. Supply-chain and monitoring are the strongest dimensions; Zero Trust is the weakest because the load-bearing boundary is an operator deployment choice the code defaults away from. The score credits the real controls and the candid threat model while holding Zero Trust down for the disclosed-but-unmitigated host-execution surface.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent is scoped to a single operator with a documented toolset and a swimlane definition, and the Desktop is architecturally non-agentic; but the toolset is deliberately broad (shell, code, browser, messaging) and domain restriction is structural/role-based rather than a hard runtime gate, so general-purpose capability remains reachable.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted external content (open web, inbound email, multi-user gateways, MCP tool output) enters the same LLM context as trusted operator instruction with only in-process heuristics distinguishing them, and the MCP description scanner that would flag poisoned tool metadata is log-only — the project's own SECURITY.md §2.6/§3.2 names this as the operator's isolation responsibility rather than a code control.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real controls exist and run — the approval gate, the un-overridable credential blocklist, the hardline floor, fail-closed API-server auth — but the default `local` backend executes LLM-emitted shell on the host, dangerous-command auto-approval fails open in non-interactive contexts (`tools/approval.py`), messaging adapters fail open absent an allowlist, and MCP tools execute without an approval gate, so the boundary that matters is an operator deployment choice the code does not enforce.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Python dependencies are exact-pinned (`==`) in `pyproject.toml` with a sha256-hash-locked `uv.lock` and enforced by supply-chain CI (`osv-scanner.yml`, `uv-lockfile-check.yml`, `supply-chain-audit.yml`), and MCP launches run an OSV malware query; minor gaps are the malware-only/fail-open OSV check and the Desktop's unpinned `curl|bash` installer from `main`.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Multiple controls cite specific adversarial findings that drove design changes — the frozen-at-import YOLO flag, the GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf credential-blocklist fix, the sudo-stdin guard, and macOS `/private/` path-bypass coverage all reference concrete attack classes — evidencing a real feedback loop, though no standalone red-team report or injection-test corpus was found in-tree.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `hermes_logging.py` writes structured, rotating `agent.log`/`errors.log`/`gateway.log` under `~/.hermes/logs/` through a `RedactingFormatter` so secrets are not persisted, and approval/auto-approval decisions are logged; the gap is that the auto-approve-on-fail-open path logs a warning rather than alerting, and there is no action-level anomaly detection.
